creator: fernape@FreeBSD.org
text:
(In reply to Li-Wen Hsu from comment #5)
I could install in a jail, but I think I know where the problem lies. I have two libc++.so.1 libraries. Apparently one of them comes from my previous FreeBSD 13.2. 

For context, I used freebsd-update to update from 13.2-RELEASE to 14.0-RELEASE.

$ /usr/local/poudriere/ports/default$ file /usr/lib/libc++.so.1 /lib/libc++.so.1 
/usr/lib/libc++.so.1: ELF 64-bit LSB shared object, x86-64, version 1 (FreeBSD), dynamically linked, for FreeBSD 13.2, stripped
/lib/libc++.so.1:     ELF 64-bit LSB shared object, x86-64, version 1 (FreeBSD), dynamically linked, for FreeBSD 14.0 (1400097), stripped

The old /usr/lib/libc++.so.1 library does not provide the symbol:

$ /usr/local/poudriere/ports/default$ nm -gD /usr/lib/libc++.so.1 | grep _ZTVNSt3__13pmr25monotonic_buffer_resourceE
$

The new library provides the symbol:

$ /usr/local/poudriere/ports/default$ nm -gD /lib/libc++.so.1 | grep _ZTVNSt3__13pmr25monotonic_buffer_resourceE
00000000001006d8 D _ZTVNSt3__13pmr25monotonic_buffer_resourceE
$

Despite this:

$ ldd /usr/local/lib/qt5/libQt5Widgets.so.5 | grep libc
        libc++.so.1 => /lib/libc++.so.1 (0x155b4c3b4000)
        libcxxrt.so.1 => /lib/libcxxrt.so.1 (0x155b4cbf4000)
        libc.so.7 => /lib/libc.so.7 (0x155b4eee3000)

that claims that libQt5Widgets.so.5 will use the new libc++ library it seems the old one somehow takes precedence. Forcing the load of the new libc++ workarounds the problem:

$ /usr/local/poudriere/ports/default$ LD_PRELOAD=/lib/libc++.so.1 keepassxc

Renaming the old /usr/lib/libc++.so.1 seems to work fine.

This somehow feels like a problem in freebsd-update(8) during the upgrade process.
I would like to close this one, but I don't want it to be lost so I will add cperciva@ and emaste@ (who fixed a problem in the update/rollback from 13.2 to 14.0).

Thank you lwhsu@ for looking into this.
